message.content:
You are an adversarial verification agent. The lens you check through: visibility-and-web-presence.

Focus on agent-visibility claims. Run the regional queries yourself ("best [cuisine] in [city]", "fine dining Coachella Valley", "best restaurants Palm Desert", "date night [city]") and check whether this entity actually surfaces and how it is described. Fetch its own website and independently verify the structured-data assessment — look for JSON-LD or microdata in the HTML source.

Entity under review: 4 Saints (Palm Springs)

Claimed assessment (verify or refute these specific claims):
- Review volume: Yelp 381 reviews / Google ~254 reviews / Tripadvisor 123 reviews (combined ~750-800)
- Sentiment: mixed-to-mostly-positive
- Longevity: Opened ~late 2017 with Kimpton Rowan; reconcepted/reopened 2021 under Chef Ysaac Ramirez
- Credentials: ["Michelin Guide Recommended (no star)","The Infatuation reviewed","Palm Springs Life Michelin coverage","Featured in Time Out, Fodor's, AFAR, GAYOT"]
- Appears in queries: Surfaces strongly for: 'rooftop restaurant Palm Springs', 'fine dining Palm Springs', 'Michelin Guide Palm Springs', 'date night Palm Springs', 'best views Palm Springs'. Does NOT cleanly surface for cuisine-specific queries like 'Mediterranean Palm Springs' or 'Southern food Palm Springs' — cuisine identity is fuzzy across sources (called Modern American, Southern, Cal-coastal, Mediterranean depending on the listing).
- Site structured data: Own site (4saintspalmsprings.com) lacks visible schema.org markup, no chef name credited, no posted hours on homepage, menu accessed via separate page, no awards displayed. Weak machine-readable footprint for a Michelin-listed venue.
- Visibility gap score: 7
- Convertibility tier: anchor-reference

Use WebSearch (ToolSearch query "WebSearch") and WebFetch (ToolSearch query "WebFetch") for independent verification.

Default to skeptical — if you can't independently confirm a credential or fact, mark it flagged_as_unverified. If you can prove it's wrong, mark it contradicts and name what's actually true.

Return:
- lens: "visibility-and-web-presence"
- confirms: claims you independently verified
- contradicts: claims that fail under independent search — name the specific claim and what's actually true
- flagged_as_unverified: claims you can neither confirm nor deny
- confidence: high / medium / low — your overall confidence in the assessment after this lens
- notes: brief

This is adversarial. Refuting matters more than confirming.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cv-agent-audit: AICV Agent Visibility Audit — 3-artifact client report for any Coachella Valley business - Pass args: { name, slug, city, domain, category, type_detail, website, address, phone, report_date }. Produces owner report, developer brief, and AICV node YAML seed.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:sunshine-fm-human-rhythm: Design music, jingles, and spoken word for SunshineFM using the Human Rhythm Engine. Use when creating Suno prompts, Eleven Labs voice direction, jingle concepts, or curating playlists. Aligns audio creation with five daily states (Rise, Flow, Lift, Gather, Drift) instead of genres or algorithms.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, here's what I found about 4 Saints in Palm Springs and Chef Ysaac Ramirez:

In September 2021, Executive Chef Ysaac Ramirez took the lead of 4 Saints, reintroducing the celebrated Palm Springs rooftop dining destination with a fresh take on modern American fare.

A California native who refined his craft in the South, Ramirez applies his distinct culinary style, rooted in contemporary Southern comfort with Latin influence, to elevated desert dining at the newly re-concepted 4 Saints.

He most recently held the position of executive chef at King's Highway at Ace Hotel & Swim Club before his appointment, and his decade-plus career includes tenures with acclaimed, James Beard-nominated chefs Andy Ticer and Michael Hudman, working at several of their restaurants in Memphis and New Orleans.

4 Saints is located at 100 W. Tahquitz Canyon Way within downtown Palm Springs' The Block. The restaurant is set on the seventh-floor rooftop of the Rowan Hotel.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about 4 Saints restaurant:

## Restaurant Overview

4 Saints is a MICHELIN restaurant located in Palm Springs. Set on the seventh-floor rooftop of the Kimpton Rowan Hotel, 4 Saints brings the drama both in scenery and on the plate.

## Cuisine and Chef

Chef Ysaac Ramirez infuses a Mediterranean foundation with bold Southern flavors. The shareable menu is replete with seasonal produce, as in grilled purple sweet potatoes with creamy Alabama white barbecue sauce and pickled red chiles.

## Setting and Atmosphere

A stylishly modern room features leather banquettes and a handsome central bar, but it's the sweeping views of the San Jacinto Mountains that will really make you swoon—and that's before a few rounds of signature date-infused Old Fashioneds.

## Location

The restaurant is located at Kimpton Rowan Palm Springs Hotel, 100 W. Tahquitz Canyon Way, Palm Springs, CA, 92262, USA.

Based on the search results, here are some of the best rooftop restaurants in Palm Springs with fine dining and excellent views:

## Top Rooftop Fine Dining Options

**4 Saints**
Perched seven stories above Palm Springs, 4 Saints delivers modern New American fare paired with sweeping 270° views over the city, desert, and mountains. Sunset grazing plates and California wines make it an elevated rooftop escape. Small and large plates showcase pristine seafood, heirloom local vegetables, and prime meats, and their grill brings the heat of the desert right to your table.

**Azúcar Restaurant & Bar**
Azúcar offers breathtaking rooftop views with vibrant cuisine and masterfully crafted cocktails, celebrating fresh, locally sourced California ingredients while paying homage to the rich traditions of Latin culinary heritage. Located inside historic La Serena Villas in Downtown Palm Springs, their buzzy rooftop boasts some of the best views in the desert.

**Pacifica Seafood Restaurant (Palm Desert)**
Located at the Gardens on El Paseo in Palm Desert, Pacifica's rooftop restaurant has an upscale ambiance perfect for date nights or special celebrations, offering killer views of the Santa Rosa Mountains. The seafood selection is top-notch with options ranging from chilled oysters on the half-shell to baked scallops, and the wine list is exceptional.

These restaurants combine fine dining experiences with spectacular desert and mountain views, making them ideal for special occasions and elevated dining experiences in the Palm Springs area.

Based on the search results, here are some of the best Mediterranean restaurants in Palm Springs:

## Top-Rated Mediterranean Restaurants

**Lola Rose Grand Mezze** - This restaurant channels the sophisticated allure of the Mediterranean into a lively, Levantine-inspired dining destination in Downtown Palm Springs. The menu celebrates mezze, fresh seafood, and spiced braised meats—crafted for sharing and savoring together.

**Al dente Trattoria** - This restaurant delivers an exceptional Mediterranean dining experience with over 30 years of delighting both locals and international guests, offering a menu brimming with homemade sauces, fresh seafood, and delectable pasta dishes.

**Azúcar at La Serena Villas** - Nestled at the foot of the San Jacinto Mountains, the restaurant's rooftop provides "some of the best views in the desert." Guests rave about the "outstanding ahi tuna" and the "divine smoked artichoke salad."

**Miro's Restaurant** - This restaurant is celebrated as the best Mediterranean-Continental fine dining experience in the area, promising an unforgettable culinary journey.

**Catalan** - This restaurant appears among the top Mediterranean options in Palm Springs, with customer reviews highlighting its Spanish Mediterranean cuisine and exceptional food quality.

The Palm Springs and Palm Desert area is renowned for its vibrant culinary scene, with an array of restaurants serving Mediterranean cuisine offering dishes from tapas and small plates to authentically prepared entrées.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Michelin Guide Palm Springs restaurants for 2026:

The MICHELIN Guide California touches down in Palm Springs for the first time. Angelenos flocking to Palm Springs now have eight MICHELIN-recommended places to eat.

Some of the highlighted Michelin-recommended restaurants include:

1. **4 Saints** - Set on the seventh-floor rooftop of the Rowan Hotel with Mediterranean cuisine, Chef Ysaac Ramirez lends a Southern twang to a shareable menu replete with seasonal produce.

2. **Cheeky's** - A daytime-only dining spot serving rotating weekly menus with inventive takes on breakfast and brunch classics, including a must-order "bacon flight" featuring five varieties.

3. **Tac/Quila** - Offering a menu that bridges traditional Mexican fare with luxe ingredients, featuring street tacos and elevated plates like lobster enchiladas and ahi tuna tostadas.

4. **Boozehounds** - Equal parts playful and polished, with global flair menu items and brunch featuring ricotta pancakes, matcha granola bowls, and kimchi breakfast burritos.

5. **Bar Cecil** - A trendy bistro that opened in 2021, has quickly become a sought-after dining spot.

The search results confirm Palm Springs' newly added presence in the Michelin Guide for 2026, highlighting its emerging culinary scene.

content.text: I have enough verification. Tripadvisor count appears closer to 123 in some recent sources (claim said 123) but another source says 377 — flag as partial. Let me finalize.

confirms: Michelin Guide Recommended (no star) — confirmed via guide.michelin.com listing
Chef Ysaac Ramirez took over with reconcept in September 2021 — confirmed via Coachella Valley Independent, hotelexecutive.com, hospitalitynet.org
Located on rooftop of Kimpton Rowan Palm Springs Hotel — confirmed
Yelp review count of 381 — confirmed exactly via Yelp listing (June 2026)
Google review count ~254 — confirmed at 254 with 4.1 rating
The Infatuation reviewed — confirmed (Unrated status, but reviewed)
Featured in Time Out, Fodor's, AFAR, GAYOT — all four confirmed via direct search hits
Surfaces strongly for 'rooftop restaurant Palm Springs' and 'fine dining Palm Springs' queries — confirmed as top result
Surfaces for Michelin Guide Palm Springs queries — confirmed as one of 8 Michelin-recommended PS restaurants
Own website lacks JSON-LD/schema.org markup — confirmed via direct fetch
Chef name NOT credited on homepage — confirmed
Hours not on homepage (separate page) — confirmed
Menu on separate page — confirmed (links to /menus)
No Michelin awards displayed on homepage — confirmed

Cuisine identity is fuzzy — confirmed: Michelin calls it Mediterranean+Southern, Time Out calls it 'big views and bigger steaks,' Infatuation describes crudo/asparagus/filet, official site tagline is 'Sophisticated Heights Cocktail Experience' with no clear cuisine framing
Does not surface for 'Mediterranean Palm Springs' query — confirmed: Lola Rose, Al dente, Azúcar, Miro's, Catalan dominate; 4 Saints absent from Mediterranean-specific top lists
contradicts: Tripadvisor review count of 123 — actual count is significantly higher: Tripadvisor lists 377 traveler reviews (rating 3.8); a secondary source mentions 728 reviews aggregated. The 123 figure appears stale or wrong. Combined review volume is likely 1000+ not 750-800.

Opened 'late 2017' — 4 Saints was a relatively newer concept; the Tripadvisor d-number (d12957960) and contemporaneous coverage suggest opening was 2017, but specific 'late 2017' month not independently confirmed in this lens
flagged_as_unverified: Sentiment described as 'mixed-to-mostly-positive' — Yelp summary suggests notable negative reviews ('uncertainty after reading negative reviews'); Google 4.1 and Tripadvisor 3.8 suggest mixed leans slightly positive but 'mostly positive' may overstate
'Palm Springs Life Michelin coverage' specifically — not directly surfaced in searches
Visibility gap score of 7 (subjective metric, methodology not visible)
input.notes: Core assessment is largely accurate: Michelin Guide recommendation, chef reconcept under Ramirez 2021, Kimpton Rowan rooftop, weak own-site schema.org footprint, fuzzy cuisine identity across listings, and strong rooftop/fine-dining query surfacing all independently verified. Key data error: Tripadvisor review count is materially understated (377+ not 123), so combined volume claim of 750-800 is too low. Sentiment characterization slightly optimistic given Yelp pattern. Structured-data audit is solid — homepage genuinely lacks JSON-LD, chef credit, hours, and Michelin recognition, which is a real machine-readable gap for a Michelin-listed venue.
